addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. - general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

1	ENGLISH FIRST DIVISION 1974/75 LEAGUE RULES
2	================================================
3	
4	Purpose
5	-------
6	These rules are intended for calculating a football league table for the
7	English First Division during the 1974/75 season.
8	
9	1. COMPETITION FORMAT
10	---------------------
11	- The division contained 22 clubs.
12	- Each club played every other club twice:
13	  - Once at home
14	  - Once away
15	- Each club therefore played 42 league matches in a complete season.
16	
17	For a partial-season table, such as the table after week 10, calculate the
18	standings only from the match results supplied. Do not assume that every team
19	has played the same number of matches.
20	
21	2. POINTS SYSTEM
22	----------------
23	The 1974/75 First Division used the following points system:
24	
25	- Win:  2 points
26	- Draw: 1 point
27	- Loss: 0 points
28	
29	Formula:
30	
31	Points = (Wins x 2) + Draws
32	
33	Example:
34	
35	Played: 10
36	Won:     5
37	Drawn:   3
38	Lost:    2
39	
40	Points = (5 x 2) + 3
41	       = 13
42	
43	3. GOAL AVERAGE
44	---------------
45	Teams level on points were separated using goal average, not goal difference.
46	
47	Goal average formula:
48	
49	Goal Average = Goals For / Goals Against
50	
51	The team with the higher goal average ranks above the other team.
52	
53	Example:
54	
55	Team A:
56	Goals For:     20
57	Goals Against: 10
58	Goal Average:  20 / 10 = 2.000
59	
60	Team B:
61	Goals For:     25
62	Goals Against: 14
63	Goal Average:  25 / 14 = 1.786
64	
65	Team A ranks above Team B because 2.000 is greater than 1.786.
66	
67	This differs from modern goal difference:
68	
69	Goal Difference = Goals For - Goals Against
70	
71	In the example above:
72	
73	Team A goal difference = +10
74	Team B goal difference = +11
75	
76	Under modern rules, Team B would rank higher. Under the 1974/75 goal-average
77	rule, Team A ranks higher.
78	
79	4. SAFE GOAL-AVERAGE COMPARISON
80	-------------------------------
81	Avoid using floating-point values when comparing goal averages because rounding
82	can produce incorrect ordering.
83	
84	To compare:
85	
86	Team A = GoalsForA / GoalsAgainstA
87	Team B = GoalsForB / GoalsAgainstB
88	
89	Cross-multiply:
90	
91	GoalsForA x GoalsAgainstB
92	
93	and compare it with:
94	
95	GoalsForB x GoalsAgainstA
96	
97	Example:
98	
99	Team A = 20 / 10
100	Team B = 25 / 14
101	
102	20 x 14 = 280
103	25 x 10 = 250
104	
105	Because 280 is greater than 250, Team A has the better goal average.
106	
107	Use a sufficiently large numeric type, such as long in Java, when multiplying.
108	
109	5. ZERO GOALS AGAINST
110	---------------------
111	A partial-season table may contain a team that has conceded zero goals.
112	
113	Recommended handling:
114	
115	- If Goals For > 0 and Goals Against = 0:
116	  Treat the goal average as positive infinity for ranking purposes.
117	
118	- If Goals For = 0 and Goals Against = 0:
119	  Handle this case explicitly as a zero-goal record.
120	
121	- Otherwise:
122	  Compare Goals For / Goals Against normally.
123	
124	Keep ranking logic separate from display formatting. The assignment's output
125	specification should determine how such a value is shown in the CSV.
126	
127	6. TABLE COLUMNS
128	----------------
129	A calculated league table will normally contain:
130	
131	- Position
132	- Team
133	- Played
134	- Won
135	- Drawn
136	- Lost
137	- Goals For
138	- Goals Against
139	- Points
140	
141	Useful abbreviations include:
142	
143	- P   = Played
144	- W   = Won
145	- D   = Drawn
146	- L   = Lost
147	- GF  = Goals For
148	- GA  = Goals Against
149	- Pts = Points
150	
151	7. UPDATING THE TABLE FROM A MATCH
152	----------------------------------
153	Example result:
154	
155	Liverpool 3-1 Arsenal
156	
157	Liverpool receives:
158	
159	- Played:       +1
160	- Won:          +1
161	- Goals For:    +3
162	- Goals Against:+1
163	- Points:       +2
164	
165	Arsenal receives:
166	
167	- Played:       +1
168	- Lost:         +1
169	- Goals For:    +1
170	- Goals Against:+3
171	- Points:       +0
172	
173	For a draw, both teams receive:
174	
175	- Played: +1
176	- Drawn:  +1
177	- Points: +1
178	
179	8. RANKING ORDER
180	----------------
181	The main ranking order should be:
182	
183	1. Points, descending
184	2. Goal average, descending (compare via cross-multiplication, never rounded floats)
185	3. Goals For, descending
186	4. Team name, ascending
187	
188	Points, goal average and goals scored were the official classification
189	criteria for the 1974/75 First Division. The alphabetical fallback is an
190	application-level decision for stable, deterministic output — it is not a
191	historical sporting rule and should be documented as such.
192	
193	9. VALIDATION RULES
194	-------------------
195	The following checks are useful for automated tests:
196	
197	For each team:
198	
199	Played = Won + Drawn + Lost
200	
201	Across the complete table:
202	
203	Total Goals For = Total Goals Against
204	
205	For all processed matches:
206	
207	Sum of Played across all teams = Number of Matches x 2
208	
209	Also verify that:
210	
211	- Scores are not negative.
212	- Team names are not blank.
213	- A team is not recorded as playing against itself.
214	- Malformed CSV rows are rejected or handled according to the specification.
215	
216	10. HISTORICAL EXAMPLE
217	----------------------
218	Derby County won the 1974/75 English First Division.
219	
220	Liverpool and Ipswich Town both finished with 51 points. Liverpool ranked above
221	Ipswich because Liverpool had the higher goal average.
222	
223	Liverpool:
224	
225	60 / 39 = approximately 1.538
226	
227	Ipswich Town:
228	
229	66 / 44 = 1.500
230	
231	This is a useful historical example of the goal-average tie-break being applied.
232	
233	11. SUMMARY
234	-----------
235	Scoring:
236	
237	- Win  = 2 points
238	- Draw = 1 point
239	- Loss = 0 points
240	
241	Ranking:
242	
243	1. Points
244	2. Goal average
245	3. Goals scored
246	4. Alphabetical (documented application-level fallback)
247	
248	Goal average:
249	
250	Goals For / Goals Against
251	
252	Do not use modern three-points-for-a-win or goal-difference rules when
253	calculating the 1974/75 English First Division standings.

file.content:
ENGLISH FIRST DIVISION 1974/75 LEAGUE RULES
================================================

Purpose
-------
These rules are intended for calculating a football league table for the
English First Division during the 1974/75 season.

1. COMPETITION FORMAT
---------------------
- The division contained 22 clubs.
- Each club played every other club twice:
  - Once at home
  - Once away
- Each club therefore played 42 league matches in a complete season.

For a partial-season table, such as the table after week 10, calculate the
standings only from the match results supplied. Do not assume that every team
has played the same number of matches.

2. POINTS SYSTEM
----------------
The 1974/75 First Division used the following points system:

- Win:  2 points
- Draw: 1 point
- Loss: 0 points

Formula:

Points = (Wins x 2) + Draws

Example:

Played: 10
Won:     5
Drawn:   3
Lost:    2

Points = (5 x 2) + 3
       = 13

3. GOAL AVERAGE
---------------
Teams level on points were separated using goal average, not goal difference.

Goal average formula:

Goal Average = Goals For / Goals Against

The team with the higher goal average ranks above the other team.

Example:

Team A:
Goals For:     20
Goals Against: 10
Goal Average:  20 / 10 = 2.000

Team B:
Goals For:     25
Goals Against: 14
Goal Average:  25 / 14 = 1.786

Team A ranks above Team B because 2.000 is greater than 1.786.

This differs from modern goal difference:

Goal Difference = Goals For - Goals Against

In the example above:

Team A goal difference = +10
Team B goal difference = +11

Under modern rules, Team B would rank higher. Under the 1974/75 goal-average
rule, Team A ranks higher.

4. SAFE GOAL-AVERAGE COMPARISON
-------------------------------
Avoid using floating-point values when comparing goal averages because rounding
can produce incorrect ordering.

To compare:

Team A = GoalsForA / GoalsAgainstA
Team B = GoalsForB / GoalsAgainstB

Cross-multiply:

GoalsForA x GoalsAgainstB

and compare it with:

GoalsForB x GoalsAgainstA

Example:

Team A = 20 / 10
Team B = 25 / 14

20 x 14 = 280
25 x 10 = 250

Because 280 is greater than 250, Team A has the better goal average.

Use a sufficiently large numeric type, such as long in Java, when multiplying.

5. ZERO GOALS AGAINST
---------------------
A partial-season table may contain a team that has conceded zero goals.

Recommended handling:

- If Goals For > 0 and Goals Against = 0:
  Treat the goal average as positive infinity for ranking purposes.

- If Goals For = 0 and Goals Against = 0:
  Handle this case explicitly as a zero-goal record.

- Otherwise:
  Compare Goals For / Goals Against normally.

Keep ranking logic separate from display formatting. The assignment's output
specification should determine how such a value is shown in the CSV.

6. TABLE COLUMNS
----------------
A calculated league table will normally contain:

- Position
- Team
- Played
- Won
- Drawn
- Lost
- Goals For
- Goals Against
- Points

Useful abbreviations include:

- P   = Played
- W   = Won
- D   = Drawn
- L   = Lost
- GF  = Goals For
- GA  = Goals Against
- Pts = Points

7. UPDATING THE TABLE FROM A MATCH
----------------------------------
Example result:

Liverpool 3-1 Arsenal

Liverpool receives:

- Played:       +1
- Won:          +1
- Goals For:    +3
- Goals Against:+1
- Points:       +2

Arsenal receives:

- Played:       +1
- Lost:         +1
- Goals For:    +1
- Goals Against:+3
- Points:       +0

For a draw, both teams receive:

- Played: +1
- Drawn:  +1
- Points: +1

8. RANKING ORDER
----------------
The main ranking order should be:

1. Points, descending
2. Goal average, descending (compare via cross-multiplication, never rounded floats)
3. Goals For, descending
4. Team name, ascending

Points, goal average and goals scored were the official classification
criteria for the 1974/75 First Division. The alphabetical fallback is an
application-level decision for stable, deterministic output — it is not a
historical sporting rule and should be documented as such.

9. VALIDATION RULES
-------------------
The following checks are useful for automated tests:

For each team:

Played = Won + Drawn + Lost

Across the complete table:

Total Goals For = Total Goals Against

For all processed matches:

Sum of Played across all teams = Number of Matches x 2

Also verify that:

- Scores are not negative.
- Team names are not blank.
- A team is not recorded as playing against itself.
- Malformed CSV rows are rejected or handled according to the specification.

10. HISTORICAL EXAMPLE
----------------------
Derby County won the 1974/75 English First Division.

Liverpool and Ipswich Town both finished with 51 points. Liverpool ranked above
Ipswich because Liverpool had the higher goal average.

Liverpool:

60 / 39 = approximately 1.538

Ipswich Town:

66 / 44 = 1.500

This is a useful historical example of the goal-average tie-break being applied.

11. SUMMARY
-----------
Scoring:

- Win  = 2 points
- Draw = 1 point
- Loss = 0 points

Ranking:

1. Points
2. Goal average
3. Goals scored
4. Alphabetical (documented application-level fallback)

Goal average:

Goals For / Goals Against

Do not use modern three-points-for-a-win or goal-difference rules when
calculating the 1974/75 English First Division standings.

We are beginning a 24-hour coding assessment. Do not implement anything yet and do not create or modify files until I approve.

Read docs/assignment.pdf and docs/rules-research.md.

Produce a requirements inventory with these sections:
1. Requirements classified by RFC 2119 keyword (MUST/SHALL, SHOULD, MAY), quoting the assignment's exact wording for each
2. For each SHOULD: state whether we will comply or deviate, and why
3. Assumptions I need to make — flag the "10th week" cutoff. My decision: all First Division matches played on or before Saturday 28 September 1974, to be documented in README with sources
4. Deliverables checklist, including every mandatory AI collaboration artifact (CLAUDE.md, .claude/, ai/, AI_REFLECTION.md, README.md, input and output CSV files)
5. Risks, ordered by how likely they are to cost me time

Keep it under a page. I will approve or amend before we proceed.

content.text:
Both documents read. Here is the requirements inventory — no files created or modified.

## 1. Requirements by RFC 2119 keyword

**MUST / MUST NOT**
- "you MUST follow these definitions" (RFC 2119 terms govern the document)
- Input/output CSV files: "You MUST include these files in your submission."
- "At SPAN we create automated tests you MUST include them in your submission."
- AI artifacts: "These artifacts are mandatory. Submissions missing any of these artifacts are incomplete and will not proceed past the coding test phase." (CLAUDE.md, .claude/, ai/ conversation history, AI_REFLECTION.md)
- "if you use some other AI you must submit equivalent artifacts" (N/A — we're using Claude Code)

**SHOULD / SHOULD NOT**
- "The input and output SHOULD be text csv files. Either using stdin/stdout or taking filenames on the command."
- "The input SHOULD contain the results of games, one per line."
- "The output should be a league standing table in the conventional format." (lowercase "should" — treat as SHOULD)
- "The rules for calculating league standings should follow those used by the English First Division in the season in question."
- "The solution should be written in one of the following languages: java, python, golang, C# (.Net Core on Linux) or scala."
- "you SHOULD NOT commit the installed packages."

**MAY / other**
- "you may need to do some research."
- "Please use platform-agnostic constructs where possible"; runs on unix-ish (OS X); document complicated setup; "Be prepared to explain your solution during a review"; production-ready CLI; use AI assistants (expectation, not keyword).

## 2. SHOULD compliance decisions

| SHOULD | Decision |
|---|---|
| CSV in/out via stdin/stdout or filename args | **Comply** — support both (filenames with stdin/stdout fallback) |
| One game result per line | **Comply** |
| Conventional league table output | **Comply** — Pos, Team, P, W, D, L, GF, GA, Pts (per rules-research §6) |
| 1974/75 First Division rules | **Comply** — 2-1-0 points, goal-average tie-break via cross-multiplication, then GF, then alphabetical (documented as an application-level fallback, not historical) |
| Listed language | **Comply** — need your pick (see below) |
| Don't commit packages | **Comply** — .gitignore already present |

**No deviations.** Deviating buys nothing here.

## 3. Assumptions to document

1. **"10th week" cutoff (your decision, flagged):** all First Division matches played **on or before Saturday 28 September 1974** — the 10th Saturday programme of the season (started 17 Aug 1974), including midweek fixtures before that date. Documented in README with sources. Note the residual risk: teams will have unequal games played (midweek variance); rules-research §1 explicitly permits this ("Do not assume that every team has played the same number of matches").
2. **Input format:** `Team A 3, Team B 1` style (SPAN's conventional format) vs. strict CSV columns — needs a decision; I'd assume the classic SPAN format `"Lions 3, Snakes 3"` unless you prefer `home,away,homeGoals,awayGoals`. **This is the one ambiguity worth settling before coding.**
3. **Goal-average display:** ranking uses exact cross-multiplication; CSV shows GA to 3 d.p. (or GF/GA columns only) — display is separate from ranking per rules-research §5.
4. Team names are canonical 1974/75 First Division names; malformed rows fail fast with a clear error (production-ready behavior).
5. **Language: not specified by you.** Recommend **Python** (zero build step on OS X, stdlib-only, fast to test) unless you want Java/Go to showcase.

## 4. Deliverables checklist

- [ ] `README.md` — setup/run instructions, week-10 cutoff rationale + sources, rules sources
- [ ] `CLAUDE.md` — project instructions for AI (root)
- [ ] `AI_REFLECTION.md` — wrong-turn or shaping-decision narrative (root)
- [ ] `.claude/` — session data committed (check .gitignore doesn't exclude it)
- [ ] `ai/` — copy of `~/.claude/projects/-Users-developer-IdeaProjects-int-span-digital-span-league-standings/` at the end
- [ ] Source code + CLI entry point

|---|---|

3. Goal-average **ranking** uses exact integer cross-multiplication; **display** in CSV is a separate formatting decision (GA=0 and 0/0 handled explicitly per rules-research §5).
4. Malformed rows are rejected with clear errors (production-ready posture); team names are canonical 1974/75 names.

Two decisions and one correction before approval.

CORRECTION — Language: your Python recommendation is rejected. The language is Java 21 — it was decided before this session (it's my daily stack and what I'd be interviewed on) with Maven Wrapper for a reproducible build, JUnit 5 + AssertJ for tests. "Zero build step" is not an advantage here: the build pipeline with quality gates is part of what I'm demonstrating. Record Java 21 + Maven in the inventory as a frozen decision.

DECISION — Input CSV row format: we use SPAN's conventional format, one match per line:
Lions 3, Snakes 1
i.e. <home team> <home score>, <away team> <away score> — team names may contain spaces, the score is the last token of each half. This matches the assignment's phrasing and the classic SPAN test convention. Note in assumptions: no header row expected; blank lines skipped; malformed rows fail fast with line numbers.

DECISION — Output format, matching convention:
1. Team Name, 13 pts
(rank, team, points with correct pluralisation "pt"/"pts") — but ALSO record as an open item: whether we print the fuller table (P W D L GF GA) or the conventional rank/points format will be finalised when we define the CSV contracts; default is the conventional SPAN format with the full statistics available.

With these applied: approved. Write the amended inventory to docs/requirements.md. Do not implement anything else yet.

  Team names may contain spaces; the score is the last token of each half.

### MUST / MUST NOT

- Input/output files: "You MUST include these files in your submission."

- "The rules for calculating league standings should follow those used by the English First Division in the season in question."

4. Team names are the canonical 1974/75 First Division names; a team never plays itself; scores are non-negative (rules-research §9 validation rules enforced).

- [ ] No committed dependencies (build output, local repo)

## 6. Risks, most likely to cost time first

1. **Sourcing/transcribing accurate week-10 1974 results** (~50–90 matches) — cross-check at least two sources.

message.content:
Next milestone: project instructions and Claude Code workspace configuration. Create exactly these four files, showing me each before writing. Do not create anything else.

1. CLAUDE.md (project root) — concise, concrete instructions:
- Objective: production-ready Java 21 CLI computing 1974/75 English First Division standings from CSV; assessment context.
- Authoritative references: docs/requirements.md (frozen decisions), docs/rules-research.md (historical rules). Never contradict them; never fabricate historical rules or data.
- Stack: Java 21, Maven Wrapper, JUnit 5 + AssertJ. No Spring, no Lombok, no speculative dependencies.
- TDD is mandatory: red, green, refactor. Never write production code without a failing test. Never delete or weaken a test to get green.
- Run ./mvnw verify after meaningful changes and before declaring any milestone complete.
- Architecture: domain (standings calculation, ranking) must not depend on CSV or CLI code.
- Ranking rules: points desc → goal average desc (integer cross-multiplication, never floats) → goals for desc → team name asc (documented app-level fallback).
- Deterministic output always. Validation failures report line numbers.
- Git: never commit without explicit approval; never rewrite history; never commit secrets or absolute local paths.
- Update docs when contracts change.

2. .claude/commands/red.md — slash command:
"Write the smallest failing JUnit test for: $ARGUMENTS. Run only that test and show the failure proves the missing behaviour. Write no production code yet."

3. .claude/commands/review.md — slash command:
"Review the current diff as a senior Java engineer. Focus: correctness vs docs/rules-research.md, comparator contract (transitivity, consistency with equals), CSV edge cases, error handling, test quality, overengineering. List issues by risk. Do not edit files."

4. .claude/settings.json — a PostToolUse hook that runs ./mvnw -q spotless:apply after Edit/Write tool calls on *.java files, so formatting is never a review comment. If the exact hook schema on this Claude Code version differs from what I've described, use the correct current schema and explain it to me.

One schema note up front: Claude Code hook matchers match **tool names only** (regex like `Edit|Write`), not file globs — so the `*.java` filtering has to happen inside the hook command itself, which reads the tool-call JSON from stdin and extracts `file_path`. **1. `CLAUDE.md`**

```markdown
# SPAN League Standings — Project Instructions

## Objective
Production-ready Java 21 CLI that computes 1974/75 English First Division league
standings from CSV match results. This is a timed coding assessment: quality of
process (TDD, small commits, documented decisions) is evaluated alongside the code.

## Authoritative references
- `docs/requirements.md` — frozen decisions (stack, cutoff date, CSV formats). 
- `docs/rules-research.md` — historical 1974/75 rules (points, goal average, validation).

Never contradict these documents. Never fabricate historical rules or match data —
if a historical fact is unknown, say so and ask.

## Stack
Java 21, Maven Wrapper (`./mvnw`), JUnit 5 + AssertJ.
No Spring, no Lombok, no speculative dependencies.

## Process
- **TDD is mandatory**: red → green → refactor. Never write production code without
  a failing test. Never delete or weaken a test to get to green.
- Run `./mvnw verify` after meaningful changes and before declaring any milestone complete.

## Architecture
- Domain layer (standings calculation, ranking) must not depend on CSV parsing or CLI code.
- Ranking: points desc → goal average desc (integer cross-multiplication, never floats)
  → goals for desc → team name asc (documented application-level fallback, not a
  historical rule).
- Output is deterministic, always. Validation failures report line numbers.

## Git
- Never commit without explicit approval. Never rewrite history.
- Never commit secrets or absolute local paths.

## Documentation
- Update `docs/` when contracts (CSV formats, CLI interface, ranking) change.
```

**2. `.claude/commands/red.md`**

```markdown
---
description: TDD red step — write the smallest failing test
---
Write the smallest failing JUnit test for: $ARGUMENTS

Run only that test and show the failure proves the missing behaviour.
Write no production code yet.
```

**3. `.claude/commands/review.md`**

```markdown
---
description: Senior-engineer review of the current diff (read-only)
---
Review the current diff as a senior Java engineer. Focus on:
- correctness vs docs/rules-research.md
- comparator contract (transitivity, consistency with equals)
- CSV edge cases
- error handling
- test quality
- overengineering

List issues ordered by risk. Do not edit files.
```

**4. `.claude/settings.json`**

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "PostToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Edit|MultiEdit|Write",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "f=$(python3 -c 'import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin).get(\"tool_input\",{}).get(\"file_path\",\"\"))' 2>/dev/null); case \"$f\" in *.java) [ -x ./mvnw ] && ./mvnw -q spotless:apply >/dev/null 2>&1 || true ;; esac; exit 0"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

How this differs from your description and why:

- **`matcher` filters by tool name, not file path.** `"Edit|MultiEdit|Write"` fires the hook on any file edit; the shell command then reads the hook payload JSON from stdin, pulls `tool_input.file_path` with python3 (present on macOS; `jq` may not be), and only runs Spotless when it ends in `.java`.
- **Guards:** `[ -x ./mvnw ]` makes the hook a no-op until the Maven Wrapper exists (it doesn't yet), and `|| true; exit 0` ensures a Spotless failure never blocks the session (a non-zero PostToolUse exit would feed noise back into the conversation).
- **Caveat to be aware of:** this runs a full `spotless:apply` (whole module) on every Java edit — a few seconds per edit once the project exists. If that gets annoying we can scope it with `-DspotlessFiles` later. Spotless itself still needs to be added to `pom.xml` when we scaffold; until then the guard keeps it silent.

Writing all four now.

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<project xmlns="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0"
         xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
         xsi:schemaLocation="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0 http://maven.apache.org/xsd/maven-4.0.0.xsd">
  <modelVersion>4.0.0</modelVersion>

  <groupId>com.spandigital</groupId>
  <artifactId>league-standings</artifactId>
  <version>1.0.0-SNAPSHOT</version>
  <packaging>jar</packaging>

  <name>league-standings</name>
  <description>CLI that calculates 1974/75 English First Division league standings from CSV match results.</description>

  <properties>
    <project.build.sourceEncoding>UTF-8</project.build.sourceEncoding>
    <maven.compiler.release>21</maven.compiler.release>

    <junit.version>5.11.4</junit.version>
    <assertj.version>3.26.3</assertj.version>

    <maven-compiler-plugin.version>3.13.0</maven-compiler-plugin.version>
    <maven-surefire-plugin.version>3.5.2</maven-surefire-plugin.version>
    <spotless.version>2.43.0</spotless.version>
    <maven-checkstyle-plugin.version>3.6.0</maven-checkstyle-plugin.version>
    <checkstyle.version>10.18.2</checkstyle.version>
    <jacoco.version>0.8.12</jacoco.version>
    <maven-jar-plugin.version>3.4.2</maven-jar-plugin.version>
  </properties>

  <dependencies>
    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.junit.jupiter</groupId>
      <artifactId>junit-jupiter</artifactId>
      <version>${junit.version}</version>
      <scope>test</scope>
    </dependency>
    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.assertj</groupId>
      <artifactId>assertj-core</artifactId>
      <version>${assertj.version}</version>
      <scope>test</scope>
    </dependency>
  </dependencies>

  <build>
    <plugins>
      <plugin>
        <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
        <artifactId>maven-compiler-plugin</artifactId>
        <version>${maven-compiler-plugin.version}</version>
        <configuration>
          <release>21</release>
        </configuration>
      </plugin>

      <plugin>
        <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
        <artifactId>maven-surefire-plugin</artifactId>
        <version>${maven-surefire-plugin.version}</version>
      </plugin>

      <plugin>
        <groupId>com.diffplug.spotless</groupId>
        <artifactId>spotless-maven-plugin</artifactId>
        <version>${spotless.version}</version>
        <configuration>
          <java>
            <palantirJavaFormat/>
          </java>
        </configuration>
        <executions>
          <execution>
            <id>spotless-check</id>
            <phase>verify</phase>
            <goals>
              <goal>check</goal>
            </goals>
          </execution>
        </executions>
      </plugin>

      <plugin>
        <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
        <artifactId>maven-checkstyle-plugin</artifactId>
        <version>${maven-checkstyle-plugin.version}</version>
        <dependencies>
          <dependency>
            <groupId>com.puppycrawl.tools</groupId>
            <artifactId>checkstyle</artifactId>
            <version>${checkstyle.version}</version>
          </dependency>
        </dependencies>
        <configuration>
          <configLocation>config/checkstyle.xml</configLocation>
          <consoleOutput>true</consoleOutput>
          <failOnViolation>true</failOnViolation>
          <includeTestSourceDirectory>true</includeTestSourceDirectory>
        </configuration>
        <executions>
          <execution>
            <id>checkstyle-check</id>
            <phase>verify</phase>
            <goals>
              <goal>check</goal>
            </goals>
          </execution>
        </executions>
      </plugin>

      <plugin>
        <groupId>org.jacoco</groupId>
        <artifactId>jacoco-maven-plugin</artifactId>
        <version>${jacoco.version}</version>
        <executions>
          <execution>
            <id>prepare-agent</id>
            <goals>
              <goal>prepare-agent</goal>
            </goals>
          </execution>
          <execution>
            <id>report</id>
            <phase>verify</phase>
            <goals>
              <goal>report</goal>
            </goals>
          </execution>
        </executions>
      </plugin>

      <plugin>
        <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
        <artifactId>maven-jar-plugin</artifactId>
        <version>${maven-jar-plugin.version}</version>
        <configuration>
          <archive>
            <manifest>
              <mainClass>com.spandigital.standings.Main</mainClass>
            </manifest>
          </archive>
        </configuration>
      </plugin>
    </plugins>
  </build>
</project>

<?xml version="1.0"?>
<!DOCTYPE module PUBLIC
    "-//Checkstyle//DTD Checkstyle Configuration 1.3//EN"
    "https://checkstyle.org/dtds/configuration_1_3.dtd">
<!--
  Pragmatic ruleset: imports, naming, modifier order, empty blocks.
  Deliberately no line-length or javadoc pedantry; formatting is Spotless's job.
-->
<module name="Checker">
  <property name="charset" value="UTF-8"/>
  <property name="severity" value="error"/>

  <module name="TreeWalker">
    <!-- Imports -->
    <module name="AvoidStarImport"/>
    <module name="RedundantImport"/>
    <module name="UnusedImports"/>
    <module name="IllegalImport"/>

    <!-- Naming -->
    <module name="PackageName"/>
    <module name="TypeName"/>
    <module name="MethodName"/>
    <module name="MemberName"/>
    <module name="ParameterName"/>
    <module name="LocalVariableName"/>
    <module name="ConstantName"/>

    <!-- Modifiers -->
    <module name="ModifierOrder"/>
    <module name="RedundantModifier"/>

    <!-- Blocks -->
    <module name="EmptyBlock"/>
    <module name="NeedBraces"/>
  </module>
</module>

--add-exports jdk.compiler/com.sun.tools.javac.api=ALL-UNNAMED
--add-exports jdk.compiler/com.sun.tools.javac.file=ALL-UNNAMED
--add-exports jdk.compiler/com.sun.tools.javac.parser=ALL-UNNAMED
--add-exports jdk.compiler/com.sun.tools.javac.tree=ALL-UNNAMED
--add-exports jdk.compiler/com.sun.tools.javac.util=ALL-UNNAMED
--add-exports jdk.compiler/com.sun.tools.javac.code=ALL-UNNAMED

Milestone complete: `./mvnw verify` is green end to end. Summary of the run:

- **Tests:** 1 run, 0 failures (SmokeTest via Surefire 3.5.2, JUnit Platform auto-detected — the silent-skip trap is confirmed not present).
- **Spotless (palantir-java-format):** 2 files clean, 0 need changes.
- **Checkstyle (10.18.2, `config/checkstyle.xml`):** 0 violations.
- **JaCoCo:** report generated (`target/site/jacoco/`), no floor yet as agreed.
- **Jar:** builds with `Main-Class: com.spandigital.standings.Main`; `java -jar` runs and exits 0.
- Cold build 22.7s (dependency downloads); warm rebuild 2.2s.

What was created: `pom.xml`, `config/checkstyle.xml`, `mvnw`/`mvnw.cmd` + `.mvn/wrapper/` (Maven 3.9.11, wrapper 3.3.4), `src/main/java/.../Main.java` (empty, private constructor), `src/test/java/.../SmokeTest.java`.

Two things worth your attention:

1. **One extra file beyond your list: `.mvn/jvm.config`** with six `--add-exports jdk.compiler/...` flags. palantir-java-format calls javac internals that JDK 16+ encapsulates; without this, Spotless dies with `IllegalAccessError` on JDK 21. It's the documented standard fix and belongs in the repo (reviewers cloning it need it too).
2. The plugin choices, one line each, as given before writing: compiler 3.13.0 (`release` 21), Surefire 3.5.2 (JUnit 5 detection), junit-jupiter 5.11.4 + AssertJ 3.26.3 test-scoped, Spotless 2.43.0 check-on-verify, Checkstyle plugin 3.6.0 with checkstyle 10.18.2 pinned (bundled default is stale), JaCoCo 0.8.12, jar plugin 3.4.2 for the manifest.

Nothing committed, per instructions. Untracked and ready for review: the above plus the milestone-2 files (`CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/`). Suggested next milestone: domain model TDD — `MatchResult`, `TeamRecord`, and the ranking comparator, starting with `/red` on the points calculation.

message.content:
Next milestone: acquire and verify the real week-10 1974/75 First Division data. No domain code in this milestone.

Context: our cutoff (frozen in docs/requirements.md) is all First Division matches played on or before Saturday 28 September 1974.

1. Write a small throwaway fetch script (scripts/fetch_results.sh or python3, your call) that downloads these date pages from englishfootballleaguetables.co.uk — one page per date the First Division played:
   http://www.englishfootballleaguetables.co.uk/season/S1974-75/1974-08-17.html
   and the same pattern for: 1974-08-20, 1974-08-21, 1974-08-24, 1974-08-27, 1974-08-28, 1974-08-31, 1974-09-07, 1974-09-14, 1974-09-21, 1974-09-24, 1974-09-25, 1974-09-28
   Cache the raw HTML under data/raw/ (gitignore data/raw/ — we keep the script, not the scrapes).

2. Extract ONLY First Division matches (each page lists all four divisions plus cups — filter carefully) into data/input/week-10-results.csv in our frozen input format:
   <home team> <home score>, <away team> <away score>
   one match per line, no header.

3. Audit the extracted file WITHOUT correcting it silently:
   - row count (expect roughly 90-110 matches for ~10 rounds of 22 teams)
   - exactly 22 unique team names, each spelled one canonical way
   - no duplicate home-away pairing (each fixture happens once per season)
   - no self-matches, no negative scores
   - per-team played counts (unequal is expected; flag anything wildly off)

4. Independent verification: fetch https://www.11v11.com/league-tables/league-division-one/28-September-1974/ and save the table it shows to docs/expected-table-28-sep-1974.md with the source URL and access date. Compare its per-team Played/GF/GA/Pts against aggregates computed from our CSV and report every discrepancy. Do not fix discrepancies yet — report them to me first.

Known sanity anchors from research: Ipswich Town top with 8 wins from ~10 games, roughly two points clear of Manchester City; QPR, Tottenham, Arsenal and Leeds level on points near the bottom.

Show me the audit report and the comparison before anything is finalised.

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Throwaway data-acquisition script for the 1974/75 week-10 dataset.
# Downloads the date pages from englishfootballleaguetables.co.uk that cover
# every First Division matchday up to the frozen cutoff (Sat 28 Sep 1974)
# and caches the raw HTML under data/raw/ (gitignored).
set -euo pipefail

cd "$(dirname "$0")/.."
mkdir -p data/raw

DATES=(
  1974-08-17
  1974-08-20
  1974-08-21
  1974-08-24
  1974-08-27
  1974-08-28
  1974-08-31
  1974-09-07
  1974-09-14
  1974-09-21
  1974-09-24
  1974-09-25
  1974-09-28
)

for d in "${DATES[@]}"; do
  out="data/raw/${d}.html"
  if [[ -s "$out" ]]; then
    echo "cached: $out"
    continue
  fi
  url="http://www.englishfootballleaguetables.co.uk/season/S1974-75/${d}.html"
  echo "fetching: $url"
  curl -fsSL --retry 3 --retry-delay 2 -o "$out" "$url"
  sleep 1
done

echo "done: $(ls data/raw/*.html | wc -l | tr -d ' ') pages cached"

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Throwaway data-acquisition script for the 1974/75 week-10 dataset.
#
# Downloads every matchday page of the 1974/75 season up to and including the
# frozen cutoff (Sat 28 Sep 1974) from englishfootballleaguetables.co.uk.
# The date list comes from the site's own season calendar
# (calendar/c1974-75.html) rather than an assumed fixture list, so midweek
# matchdays cannot be silently missed. Each page lists all divisions and cups;
# filtering to Division 1 happens in scripts/extract_results.py.
#
# The live site returns 502 as of 2026-07-11, so each fetch falls back to the
# Wayback Machine ("id_" = unmodified original markup). Raw HTML is cached
# under data/raw/ (gitignored — we keep the script, not the scrapes).
set -euo pipefail

cd "$(dirname "$0")/.."
mkdir -p data/raw

DATES=(
  1974-08-03 1974-08-06 1974-08-07 1974-08-10
  1974-08-17 1974-08-19 1974-08-20 1974-08-21 1974-08-23 1974-08-24
  1974-08-27 1974-08-28 1974-08-30 1974-08-31
  1974-09-02 1974-09-03 1974-09-04 1974-09-05 1974-09-06 1974-09-07
  1974-09-09 1974-09-10 1974-09-11 1974-09-13 1974-09-14
  1974-09-16 1974-09-17 1974-09-18 1974-09-20 1974-09-21
  1974-09-23 1974-09-24 1974-09-25 1974-09-26 1974-09-27 1974-09-28
)

for d in "${DATES[@]}"; do
  out="data/raw/${d}.html"
  if [[ -s "$out" ]]; then
    echo "cached:  $out"
    continue
  fi
  live="http://www.englishfootballleaguetables.co.uk/season/S1974-75/${d}.html"
  wayback="http://web.archive.org/web/2020id_/http://englishfootballleaguetables.co.uk/season/S1974-75/${d}.html"
  if curl -fsSL --max-time 30 -o "$out" "$live" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "live:    $out"
  elif curl -fsSL --retry 3 --retry-delay 2 --max-time 90 -o "$out" "$wayback"; then
    echo "wayback: $out"
  else
    echo "MISSING: $d (not on live site or Wayback)" >&2
    rm -f "$out"
  fi
  sleep 1
done

echo "done: $(ls data/raw/*.html 2>/dev/null | wc -l | tr -d ' ') of ${#DATES[@]} pages cached"

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Throwaway data-acquisition script for the 1974/75 week-10 dataset.
# Downloads the date pages from englishfootballleaguetables.co.uk that cover
# every First Division matchday up to the frozen cutoff (Sat 28 Sep 1974)
# and caches the raw HTML under data/raw/ (gitignored).
set -euo pipefail

cd "$(dirname "$0")/.."
mkdir -p data/raw

DATES=(
  1974-08-17
  1974-08-20
  1974-08-21
  1974-08-24
  1974-08-27
  1974-08-28
  1974-08-31
  1974-09-07
  1974-09-14
  1974-09-21
  1974-09-24
  1974-09-25
  1974-09-28
)

for d in "${DATES[@]}"; do
  out="data/raw/${d}.html"
  if [[ -s "$out" ]]; then
    echo "cached: $out"
    continue
  fi
  url="http://www.englishfootballleaguetables.co.uk/season/S1974-75/${d}.html"
  echo "fetching: $url"
  curl -fsSL --retry 3 --retry-delay 2 -o "$out" "$url"
  sleep 1
done

echo "done: $(ls data/raw/*.html | wc -l | tr -d ' ') pages cached"

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Throwaway data-acquisition script for the 1974/75 week-10 dataset.
#
# Source history (2026-07-11):
#   1. englishfootballleaguetables.co.uk (primary plan) — entire site returns
#      502 and its /season/ date pages were never archived by the Wayback
#      Machine. Dead end, but its archived season calendar validated the
#      matchday date list.
#   2. 11v11.com, worldfootball.net, fbref.com — all return 403 to
#      non-browser clients and have no usable Wayback snapshots of the
#      pages we need.
#   3. statto.com (defunct since 2017) — well archived. Its
#      /division-one-old/1974-1975/results/<date> pages list ONLY First
#      Division matches for that date (division filtering is in the URL,
#      not in page content), and /table/full/1974-09-28 provides a
#      cross-check league table dated exactly at our cutoff.
#
# Dates: every First Division matchday on or before Sat 28 Sep 1974 for
# which Statto has a results page (14 dates — includes Mon 1974-08-19,
# which the original hand-researched list missed).
#
# Raw HTML is cached under data/raw/ (gitignored — we keep the script,
# not the scrapes). "id_" in Wayback URLs = unmodified original markup.
set -euo pipefail

cd "$(dirname "$0")/.."
mkdir -p data/raw

STATTO="http://www.statto.com/football/stats/england/division-one-old/1974-1975"
WAYBACK="http://web.archive.org/web/2012id_"

DATES=(
  1974-08-17 1974-08-19 1974-08-20 1974-08-21 1974-08-24
  1974-08-27 1974-08-28 1974-08-31
  1974-09-07 1974-09-14 1974-09-21 1974-09-24 1974-09-25 1974-09-28
)

for d in "${DATES[@]}"; do
  out="data/raw/statto-results-${d}.html"
  if [[ -s "$out" ]]; then
    echo "cached:  $out"
    continue
  fi
  # -L: Wayback redirects the approximate timestamp to the nearest snapshot
  curl -fsSL --retry 3 --retry-delay 2 --max-time 90 \
    -o "$out" "${WAYBACK}/${STATTO}/results/${d}" || {
      echo "MISSING: $d" >&2; rm -f "$out"; }
  sleep 1
done

table_out="data/raw/statto-table-full-1974-09-28.html"
if [[ ! -s "$table_out" ]]; then
  curl -fsSL --retry 3 --retry-delay 2 --max-time 90 \
    -o "$table_out" "${WAYBACK}/${STATTO}/table/full/1974-09-28"
fi

echo "done: $(ls data/raw/statto-*.html | wc -l | tr -d ' ') pages cached"

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Throwaway data-acquisition script for the 1974/75 week-10 dataset.
#
# Downloads every matchday page of the 1974/75 season up to and including the
# frozen cutoff (Sat 28 Sep 1974) from englishfootballleaguetables.co.uk.
# The date list comes from the site's own season calendar
# (calendar/c1974-75.html) rather than an assumed fixture list, so midweek
# matchdays cannot be silently missed. Each page lists all divisions and cups;
# filtering to Division 1 happens in scripts/extract_results.py.
#
# The live site returns 502 as of 2026-07-11, so each fetch falls back to the
# Wayback Machine ("id_" = unmodified original markup). Raw HTML is cached
# under data/raw/ (gitignored — we keep the script, not the scrapes).
set -euo pipefail

cd "$(dirname "$0")/.."
mkdir -p data/raw

DATES=(
  1974-08-03 1974-08-06 1974-08-07 1974-08-10
  1974-08-17 1974-08-19 1974-08-20 1974-08-21 1974-08-23 1974-08-24
  1974-08-27 1974-08-28 1974-08-30 1974-08-31
  1974-09-02 1974-09-03 1974-09-04 1974-09-05 1974-09-06 1974-09-07
  1974-09-09 1974-09-10 1974-09-11 1974-09-13 1974-09-14
  1974-09-16 1974-09-17 1974-09-18 1974-09-20 1974-09-21
  1974-09-23 1974-09-24 1974-09-25 1974-09-26 1974-09-27 1974-09-28
)

for d in "${DATES[@]}"; do
  out="data/raw/${d}.html"
  if [[ -s "$out" ]]; then
    echo "cached:  $out"
    continue
  fi
  live="http://www.englishfootballleaguetables.co.uk/season/S1974-75/${d}.html"
  wayback="http://web.archive.org/web/2020id_/http://englishfootballleaguetables.co.uk/season/S1974-75/${d}.html"
  if curl -fsSL --max-time 30 -o "$out" "$live" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "live:    $out"
  elif curl -fsSL --retry 3 --retry-delay 2 --max-time 90 -o "$out" "$wayback"; then
    echo "wayback: $out"
  else
    echo "MISSING: $d (not on live site or Wayback)" >&2
    rm -f "$out"
  fi
  sleep 1
done

echo "done: $(ls data/raw/*.html 2>/dev/null | wc -l | tr -d ' ') of ${#DATES[@]} pages cached"

    cells = [re.sub(r'<[^>]+>', '', c).strip() for c in re.findall(r'<t[dh][^>]*>(.*?)</t[dh]>', r, re.S)]
    if len(cells) > 8 and any('Ipswich' in c or c == 'P' for c in cells):
        print(cells)
EOF

input.content: #!/usr/bin/env python3 """Throwaway extractor: Statto date pages -> data/input/week-10-results.csv. Each data/raw/statto-results-.html page lists ONLY First Division matches for that date (the division filter is part of the Statto URL), so no cross-division filtering is needed here. Match rows are elements whose middle cell is a full-time score like "3-1". Output uses the frozen input format from docs/requirements.md: , one match per line, no header, in date order then page order. """ import glob import re import sys from pathlib import Path RAW_GLOB = "data/raw/statto-results-*.html" OUT_PATH = Path("data/input/week-10-results.csv") MONTHS = { "January": 1, "February": 2, "March": 3, "April": 4, "May": 5, "June": 6, "July": 7, "August": 8, "September": 9, "October": 10, "November": 11, "December": 12, } def page_date(html: str, path: str) -> str: """Return the ISO date the page claims to show, from its .""" m = re.search(r"<title>[^<]*?(\d{1,2})(?:st|nd|rd|th) (\w+) (\d{4})", html) if not m: sys.exit(f"ERROR: no date in <title> of {path}") day, month, year = int(m.group(1)), MONTHS[m.group(2)], int(m.group(3)) return f"{year:04d}-{month:02d}-{day:02d}" def extract_matches(html: str) -> list[tuple[str, int, str, int]]: matches = [] for row in re.findall(r"<tr[^>]*>(.*?)</tr>", html, re.S): cells = [ re.sub(r"<[^>]+>", "", c).strip() for c in re.findall(r"<td[^>]*>(.*?)</td>", row, re.S) ] for i, cell in enumerate(cells): score = re.fullmatch(r"(\d+)-(\d+)", cell) if score and 0 < i < len(cells) - 1 and cells[i - 1] and cells[i + 1]: matches.append(( cells[i - 1], int(score.group(1)), cells[i + 1], int(score.group(2)), )) break return matches def main() -> None: pages = sorted(glob.glob(RAW_GLOB)) if not pages: sys.exit(f"ERROR: no raw pages match {RAW_GLOB}; run scripts/fetch_results.sh") lines = [] for path in pages: if "table" in path: continue html = open(path, encoding="utf-8", errors="replace").read() expected = re.search(r"(\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2})", path).group(1) actual = page_date(html, path) if actual != expected: sys.exit(f"ERROR: {path} claims {actual}, expected {expected} " "(Wayback may have redirected to a different page)") matches = extract_matches(html) if not matches: sys.exit(f"ERROR: no matches extracted from {path}") print(f"{expected}: {len(matches)} matches") lines += [f"{h} {hs}, {a} {as_}" for h, hs, a, as_ in matches] OUT_PATH.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) OUT_PATH.write_text("\n".join(lines) + "\n", encoding="utf-8") print(f"wrote {len(lines)} matches to {OUT_PATH}") if __name__ == "__main__": main()

input.content: #!/usr/bin/env python3 """Throwaway auditor for data/input/week-10-results.csv. Reports (never fixes): - row count - unique team names (expect exactly 22, one canonical spelling each) - duplicate home-away pairings (each fixture is played once per season) - self-matches and negative scores - per-team played counts (unequal is expected; wild outliers flagged) - aggregate P/W/D/L/GF/GA/Pts per team, compared against the Statto "table on 28 Sep 1974" page cached in data/raw/ — every discrepancy is reported. """ import re import sys from collections import Counter, defaultdict from pathlib import Path IN_PATH = Path("data/input/week-10-results.csv") TABLE_PATH = Path("data/raw/statto-table-full-1974-09-28.html") LINE_RE = re.compile(r"^(.*\S) (\d+), (.*\S) (\d+)$") def parse_matches(): matches = [] for n, line in enumerate(IN_PATH.read_text(encoding="utf-8").splitlines(), 1): if not line.strip(): continue m = LINE_RE.match(line) if not m: print(f"MALFORMED line {n}: {line!r}") continue matches.append((n, m.group(1), int(m.group(2)), m.group(3), int(m.group(4)))) return matches def parse_reference_table(): html = TABLE_PATH.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace") ref = {} for row in re.findall(r"]*>(.*?)", html, re.S): cells = [ re.sub(r"<[^>]+>", "", c).strip() for c in re.findall(r"]*>(.*?)", row, re.S) ] if len(cells) >= 11 and cells[0].isdigit() and cells[2].isdigit(): pos, team, p, w, d, l, f, a = cells[0], cells[1], *map(int, cells[2:8]) pts = int(cells[10]) ref[team] = dict(pos=int(pos), p=p, w=w, d=d, l=l, gf=f, ga=a, pts=pts) return ref def main(): matches = parse_matches() print(f"== row count ==\n{len(matches)} matches\n") teams = Counter() stats = defaultdict(lambda: dict(p=0, w=0, d=0, l=0, gf=0, ga=0, pts=0)) pairings = Counter() problems = [] for n, home, hs, away, as_, in matches: teams[home] += 1 teams[away] += 1 pairings[(home, away)] += 1 if home == away: problems.append(f"line {n}: self-match {home!r}") if hs < 0 or as_ < 0: problems.append(f"line {n}: negative score") for team, gf, ga in ((home, hs, as_), (away, as_, hs)): s = stats[team] s["p"] += 1 s["gf"] += gf s["ga"] += ga if gf > ga: s["w"] += 1 s["pts"] += 2 elif gf == ga: s["d"] += 1 s["pts"] += 1 else: s["l"] += 1 print(f"== unique teams ==\n{len(teams)} teams") for t in sorted(teams): print(f" {t} (appears {teams[t]}x)") print() dupes = {k: v for k, v in pairings.items() if v > 1} print("== duplicate home-away pairings ==") print("\n".join(f" {k}: {v}x" for k, v in dupes.items()) or " none") print("\n== self-matches / negative scores ==") print("\n".join(f" {p}" for p in problems) or " none") played = sorted((s["p"], t) for t, s in stats.items()) print("\n== per-team played counts ==") for p, t in played: print(f" {p} {t}") print("\n== comparison vs Statto table on 28 Sep 1974 ==") ref = parse_reference_table() print(f"reference rows parsed: {len(ref)}") discrepancies = 0 for team in sorted(set(ref) | set(stats)): if team not in ref: print(f" DISCREPANCY: {team} in CSV but not in reference table") discrepancies += 1 continue if team not in stats: print(f" DISCREPANCY: {team} in reference table but not in CSV") discrepancies += 1 continue r, s = ref[team], stats[team] for k in ("p", "w", "d", "l", "gf", "ga", "pts"): if r[k] != s[k]: print(f" DISCREPANCY: {team} {k.upper()}: CSV={s[k]} ref={r[k]}") discrepancies += 1 if discrepancies == 0: print(" all 7 aggregate columns match for every team") else: print(f" TOTAL: {discrepancies} discrepancies") sys.exit(1) if __name__ == "__main__": main()

python3 - <<'EOF'
import re
html = open('data/raw/11v11-table-1974-09-07.html', encoding='utf-8', errors='replace').read()
t = re.search(r'<title>([^<]*)</title>', html)
print('TITLE:', t.group(1) if t else '?')
rows = re.findall(r'<tr[^>]*>(.*?)</tr>', html, re.S)
for r in rows[:6]:
    cells = [re.sub(r'<[^>]+>', '', c).strip() for c in re.findall(r'<t[dh][^>]*>(.*?)</t[dh]>', r, re.S)]
    if cells: print(cells)
EOF

# Expected First Division table — Saturday 28 September 1974

Reference table used to verify `data/input/week-10-results.csv` (the frozen
"week 10" cutoff: all First Division matches played on or before Sat 28 Sep
1974, per `docs/requirements.md`).

## Source

- **Statto** (statto.com, site defunct since 2017), page titled
  *"English Division One (old) Table on Saturday 28th September 1974"*,
  via the Wayback Machine:
  `http://web.archive.org/web/20160401123205/http://www.statto.com/football/stats/england/division-one-old/1974-1975/table/full/1974-09-28`
- Accessed: 2026-07-11.

The originally planned source, `https://www.11v11.com/league-tables/league-division-one/28-September-1974/`,
was unreachable at access time: the live site returns HTTP 403 to
non-browser clients, that URL has no Wayback snapshot, and a save-page
request to the Wayback Machine failed (HTTP 520). See "Independent
cross-check" below for how 11v11 was still used.

## Table as shown by the source

| Pos | Team | P | W | D | L | GF | GA | GAvg | Pts |
|----:|------|--:|--:|--:|--:|---:|---:|-----:|----:|
| 1 | Ipswich Town | 10 | 8 | 0 | 2 | 18 | 6 | 3.00 | 16 |
| 2 | Manchester City | 10 | 6 | 2 | 2 | 14 | 11 | 1.27 | 14 |
| 3 | Liverpool | 10 | 6 | 1 | 3 | 17 | 8 | 2.12 | 13 |
| 4 | Everton | 10 | 4 | 5 | 1 | 14 | 11 | 1.27 | 13 |
| 5 | Sheffield United | 10 | 5 | 3 | 2 | 14 | 14 | 1.00 | 13 |
| 6 | Newcastle United | 9 | 5 | 2 | 2 | 16 | 13 | 1.23 | 12 |
| 7 | Middlesbrough | 9 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 12 | 7 | 1.71 | 11 |
| 8 | Derby County | 10 | 3 | 5 | 2 | 16 | 13 | 1.23 | 11 |
| 9 | Stoke City | 10 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 13 | 11 | 1.18 | 11 |
| 10 | Wolverhampton Wndrs | 10 | 3 | 5 | 2 | 12 | 11 | 1.09 | 11 |
| 11 | Carlisle United | 10 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 8 | 8 | 1.00 | 10 |
| 12 | West Ham United | 10 | 4 | 1 | 5 | 20 | 18 | 1.11 | 9 |
| 13 | Burnley | 10 | 4 | 1 | 5 | 17 | 18 | 0.94 | 9 |
| 14 | Birmingham City | 10 | 3 | 2 | 5 | 12 | 17 | 0.71 | 8 |
| 15 | Coventry City | 10 | 2 | 4 | 4 | 11 | 17 | 0.65 | 8 |
| 16 | Leicester City | 9 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 13 | 17 | 0.77 | 7 |
| 17 | Luton Town | 10 | 1 | 5 | 4 | 11 | 16 | 0.69 | 7 |
| 18 | Chelsea | 10 | 2 | 3 | 5 | 10 | 18 | 0.56 | 7 |
| 19 | Leeds United | 9 | 2 | 2 | 5 | 12 | 14 | 0.86 | 6 |
| 20 | Arsenal | 9 | 2 | 2 | 5 | 9 | 12 | 0.75 | 6 |
| 21 | Tottenham Hotspur | 9 | 3 | 0 | 6 | 11 | 15 | 0.73 | 6 |
| 22 | Queens Park Rangers | 10 | 1 | 4 | 5 | 8 | 13 | 0.61 | 6 |

Unequal Played counts (9 vs 10) are correct: six clubs had a postponed or
not-yet-played fixture as of the cutoff.

## Verification performed (2026-07-11, `scripts/audit_results.py`)

1. **Aggregates vs this table**: P, W, D, L, GF, GA, Pts computed from
   `data/input/week-10-results.csv` match this table exactly — all 22 teams,
   all 7 columns, zero discrepancies.
2. **Independent cross-check (different site)**: because results and this
   table share a source (Statto), the dataset's prefix up to 7 Sep 1974
   (66 matches) was additionally compared against 11v11's archived table
   dated 7 September 1974
   (`http://web.archive.org/web/20201213215254/https://www.11v11.com/league-tables/league-division-one/07-september-1974/`,
   accessed 2026-07-11): exact match, all 22 teams, all 7 columns.
3. **Sanity anchors from prior research** (docs/assignment research):
   Ipswich Town top with 8 wins from 10, two points clear of Manchester
   City; QPR, Tottenham, Arsenal and Leeds level on 6 points at the
   bottom — all confirmed by this table.

## Naming note

Statto spells one club "Wolverhampton Wndrs" (11v11: "Wolverhampton
Wanderers"). The input CSV currently uses the Statto spelling; the
canonical display name is an open decision for the CSV contract.

content.text:
Milestone complete — the dataset is built, audited, and verified with zero discrepancies. Here's the full report, including two sourcing decisions I had to make when the planned sources turned out to be unreachable.

## Sourcing: the plan changed, provenance is documented

- **englishfootballleaguetables.co.uk is dead** — the whole site returns 502, and its `/season/` date pages were never archived by the Wayback Machine (I checked all 14,066 archived URLs for the domain). Its archived season calendar was still useful: it validated the matchday date list.
- **11v11, worldfootball, FBref all return 403** to any non-browser client (curl, WebFetch), and none has a Wayback snapshot of the pages we need. A live save-page request to archive.org also failed.
- **Solution: Statto** (defunct since 2017, thoroughly archived). Its `/division-one-old/1974-1975/results/<date>` pages list *only* First Division matches — division filtering is in the URL, not page content — which eliminates the cross-division extraction risk entirely. It also has a table page dated exactly **Saturday 28th September 1974**.
- **Bonus find:** Statto has a matchday on **Mon 19 Aug 1974** (one match) that the original 13-date list missed. It's included; the fetch script documents this.

## Audit report (`scripts/audit_results.py`, nothing corrected silently)

- **107 matches** extracted into `data/input/week-10-results.csv` (within the expected 90–110 range).
- **Exactly 22 unique team names**, each spelled one way.
- **No duplicate home-away pairings, no self-matches, no negative scores.**
- **Played counts:** sixteen clubs on 10, six clubs on 9 (Arsenal, Leeds, Leicester, Middlesbrough, Newcastle, Tottenham) — consistent with postponed fixtures, and confirmed by the reference table showing the same six clubs on P=9.

## Verification — zero discrepancies

1. **vs Statto table dated 28 Sep 1974:** P/W/D/L/GF/GA/Pts computed from our CSV match exactly, all 22 teams × 7 columns. Saved with source URLs and access date in `docs/expected-table-28-sep-1974.md`.
2. **Independent cross-check (different site):** since results and table above share a source, I also compared our dataset's prefix up to 7 Sep (66 matches) against **11v11's archived table dated 7 September 1974** — exact match, all 22 teams × 7 columns.
3. **Your sanity anchors all confirmed:** Ipswich top, 8 wins from 10, two points clear of Man City; QPR/Spurs/Arsenal/Leeds all on 6 points at the bottom.

## One decision for you

Statto (and therefore our CSV) spells one club **"Wolverhampton Wndrs"**; 11v11 uses "Wolverhampton Wanderers". Flagged in the expected-table doc — do you want the CSV normalised to the full name when we define the CSV contracts, or kept verbatim from the source?

Files: `scripts/fetch_results.sh` (rewritten for the Wayback/Statto strategy, full source history in comments), `scripts/extract_results.py`, `scripts/audit_results.py`, `data/input/week-10-results.csv`, `docs/expected-table-28-sep-1974.md`. Raw HTML cached in `data/raw/` (gitignored). Nothing committed.

message.content:
Decision on the name variant: normalise "Wolverhampton Wndrs" to "Wolverhampton Wanderers" in the extraction step, via an explicit, visible mapping in the extract script (not a silent string replace buried in parsing). Rationale: the output is a conventional league table, and the club's canonical name is Wolverhampton Wanderers — "Wndrs" is a display abbreviation from Statto's layout, not a name. Record the normalisation in docs/expected-table-28-sep-1974.md provenance notes and later in the README data section.

Re-run the extraction and audit to confirm 107 matches / 22 teams still hold with the normalised name, then show me git status. After I confirm, stage and commit: scripts/, data/input/week-10-results.csv, docs/expected-table-28-sep-1974.md — plus the .gitignore change for data/raw/ if not already tracked. Suggested message: "data: add verified week-10 1974-75 First Division results with provenance"

toolUseResult.originalFile: #!/usr/bin/env python3 """Throwaway extractor: Statto date pages -> data/input/week-10-results.csv. Each data/raw/statto-results-.html page lists ONLY First Division matches for that date (the division filter is part of the Statto URL), so no cross-division filtering is needed here. Match rows are elements whose middle cell is a full-time score like "3-1". Output uses the frozen input format from docs/requirements.md: , one match per line, no header, in date order then page order. """ import glob import re import sys from pathlib import Path RAW_GLOB = "data/raw/statto-results-*.html" OUT_PATH = Path("data/input/week-10-results.csv") MONTHS = { "January": 1, "February": 2, "March": 3, "April": 4, "May": 5, "June": 6, "July": 7, "August": 8, "September": 9, "October": 10, "November": 11, "December": 12, } def page_date(html: str, path: str) -> str: """Return the ISO date the page claims to show, from its .""" m = re.search(r"<title>[^<]*?(\d{1,2})(?:st|nd|rd|th) (\w+) (\d{4})", html) if not m: sys.exit(f"ERROR: no date in <title> of {path}") day, month, year = int(m.group(1)), MONTHS[m.group(2)], int(m.group(3)) return f"{year:04d}-{month:02d}-{day:02d}" def extract_matches(html: str) -> list[tuple[str, int, str, int]]: matches = [] for row in re.findall(r"<tr[^>]*>(.*?)</tr>", html, re.S): cells = [ re.sub(r"<[^>]+>", "", c).strip() for c in re.findall(r"<td[^>]*>(.*?)</td>", row, re.S) ] for i, cell in enumerate(cells): score = re.fullmatch(r"(\d+)-(\d+)", cell) if score and 0 < i < len(cells) - 1 and cells[i - 1] and cells[i + 1]: matches.append(( cells[i - 1], int(score.group(1)), cells[i + 1], int(score.group(2)), )) break return matches def main() -> None: pages = sorted(glob.glob(RAW_GLOB)) if not pages: sys.exit(f"ERROR: no raw pages match {RAW_GLOB}; run scripts/fetch_results.sh") lines = [] for path in pages: if "table" in path: continue html = open(path, encoding="utf-8", errors="replace").read() expected = re.search(r"(\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2})", path).group(1) actual = page_date(html, path) if actual != expected: sys.exit(f"ERROR: {path} claims {actual}, expected {expected} " "(Wayback may have redirected to a different page)") matches = extract_matches(html) if not matches: sys.exit(f"ERROR: no matches extracted from {path}") print(f"{expected}: {len(matches)} matches") lines += [f"{h} {hs}, {a} {as_}" for h, hs, a, as_ in matches] OUT_PATH.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) OUT_PATH.write_text("\n".join(lines) + "\n", encoding="utf-8") print(f"wrote {len(lines)} matches to {OUT_PATH}") if __name__ == "__main__": main()

Each data/raw/statto-results-.html page lists ONLY First Division matches for that date (the division filter is part of the Statto URL), so no cross-division filtering is needed here. Match rows are elements whose middle cell is a full-time score like "3-1". Output uses the frozen input format from docs/requirements.md: , one match per line, no header, in date order then page order. """ import glob import re import sys from pathlib import Path RAW_GLOB = "data/raw/statto-results-*.html" OUT_PATH = Path("data/input/week-10-results.csv") MONTHS = { "January": 1, "February": 2, "March": 3, "April": 4, "May": 5, "June": 6, "July": 7, "August": 8, "September": 9, "October": 10, "November": 11, "December": 12, } # Statto's layout abbreviates some club names; the dataset uses canonical # club names (decision 2026-07-11, recorded in docs/expected-table-28-sep-1974.md). NAME_NORMALISATIONS = { "Wolverhampton Wndrs": "Wolverhampton Wanderers", } def page_date(html: str, path: str) -> str: """Return the ISO date the page claims to show, from its .""" m = re.search(r"<title>[^<]*?(\d{1,2})(?:st|nd|rd|th) (\w+) (\d{4})", html) if not m: sys.exit(f"ERROR: no date in <title> of {path}") day, month, year = int(m.group(1)), MONTHS[m.group(2)], int(m.group(3)) return f"{year:04d}-{month:02d}-{day:02d}" def extract_matches(html: str) -> list[tuple[str, int, str, int]]: matches = [] for row in re.findall(r"<tr[^>]*>(.*?)</tr>", html, re.S): cells = [ re.sub(r"<[^>]+>", "", c).strip() for c in re.findall(r"<td[^>]*>(.*?)</td>", row, re.S) ] for i, cell in enumerate(cells): score = re.fullmatch(r"(\d+)-(\d+)", cell) if score and 0 < i < len(cells) - 1 and cells[i - 1] and cells[i + 1]: matches.append(( cells[i - 1], int(score.group(1)), cells[i + 1], int(score.group(2)), )) break return matches def main() -> None: pages = sorted(glob.glob(RAW_GLOB)) if not pages: sys.exit(f"ERROR: no raw pages match {RAW_GLOB}; run scripts/fetch_results.sh") lines = [] for path in pages: if "table" in path: continue html = open(path, encoding="utf-8", errors="replace").read() expected = re.search(r"(\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2})", path).group(1) actual = page_date(html, path) if actual != expected: sys.exit(f"ERROR: {path} claims {actual}, expected {expected} " "(Wayback may have redirected to a different page)") matches = extract_matches(html) if not matches: sys.exit(f"ERROR: no matches extracted from {path}") print(f"{expected}: {len(matches)} matches") lines += [f"{h} {hs}, {a} {as_}" for h, hs, a, as_ in matches] OUT_PATH.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) OUT_PATH.write_text("\n".join(lines) + "\n", encoding="utf-8") print(f"wrote {len(lines)} matches to {OUT_PATH}") if __name__ == "__main__": main()

toolUseResult.originalFile: #!/usr/bin/env python3 """Throwaway auditor for data/input/week-10-results.csv. Reports (never fixes): - row count - unique team names (expect exactly 22, one canonical spelling each) - duplicate home-away pairings (each fixture is played once per season) - self-matches and negative scores - per-team played counts (unequal is expected; wild outliers flagged) - aggregate P/W/D/L/GF/GA/Pts per team, compared against the Statto "table on 28 Sep 1974" page cached in data/raw/ — every discrepancy is reported. """ import re import sys from collections import Counter, defaultdict from pathlib import Path IN_PATH = Path("data/input/week-10-results.csv") TABLE_PATH = Path("data/raw/statto-table-full-1974-09-28.html") LINE_RE = re.compile(r"^(.*\S) (\d+), (.*\S) (\d+)$") def parse_matches(): matches = [] for n, line in enumerate(IN_PATH.read_text(encoding="utf-8").splitlines(), 1): if not line.strip(): continue m = LINE_RE.match(line) if not m: print(f"MALFORMED line {n}: {line!r}") continue matches.append((n, m.group(1), int(m.group(2)), m.group(3), int(m.group(4)))) return matches def parse_reference_table(): html = TABLE_PATH.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace") ref = {} for row in re.findall(r"]*>(.*?)", html, re.S): cells = [ re.sub(r"<[^>]+>", "", c).strip() for c in re.findall(r"]*>(.*?)", row, re.S) ] if len(cells) >= 11 and cells[0].isdigit() and cells[2].isdigit(): pos, team, p, w, d, l, f, a = cells[0], cells[1], *map(int, cells[2:8]) pts = int(cells[10]) ref[team] = dict(pos=int(pos), p=p, w=w, d=d, l=l, gf=f, ga=a, pts=pts) return ref def main(): matches = parse_matches() print(f"== row count ==\n{len(matches)} matches\n") teams = Counter() stats = defaultdict(lambda: dict(p=0, w=0, d=0, l=0, gf=0, ga=0, pts=0)) pairings = Counter() problems = [] for n, home, hs, away, as_, in matches: teams[home] += 1 teams[away] += 1 pairings[(home, away)] += 1 if home == away: problems.append(f"line {n}: self-match {home!r}") if hs < 0 or as_ < 0: problems.append(f"line {n}: negative score") for team, gf, ga in ((home, hs, as_), (away, as_, hs)): s = stats[team] s["p"] += 1 s["gf"] += gf s["ga"] += ga if gf > ga: s["w"] += 1 s["pts"] += 2 elif gf == ga: s["d"] += 1 s["pts"] += 1 else: s["l"] += 1 print(f"== unique teams ==\n{len(teams)} teams") for t in sorted(teams): print(f" {t} (appears {teams[t]}x)") print() dupes = {k: v for k, v in pairings.items() if v > 1} print("== duplicate home-away pairings ==") print("\n".join(f" {k}: {v}x" for k, v in dupes.items()) or " none") print("\n== self-matches / negative scores ==") print("\n".join(f" {p}" for p in problems) or " none") played = sorted((s["p"], t) for t, s in stats.items()) print("\n== per-team played counts ==") for p, t in played: print(f" {p} {t}") print("\n== comparison vs Statto table on 28 Sep 1974 ==") ref = parse_reference_table() print(f"reference rows parsed: {len(ref)}") discrepancies = 0 for team in sorted(set(ref) | set(stats)): if team not in ref: print(f" DISCREPANCY: {team} in CSV but not in reference table") discrepancies += 1 continue if team not in stats: print(f" DISCREPANCY: {team} in reference table but not in CSV") discrepancies += 1 continue r, s = ref[team], stats[team] for k in ("p", "w", "d", "l", "gf", "ga", "pts"): if r[k] != s[k]: print(f" DISCREPANCY: {team} {k.upper()}: CSV={s[k]} ref={r[k]}") discrepancies += 1 if discrepancies == 0: print(" all 7 aggregate columns match for every team") else: print(f" TOTAL: {discrepancies} discrepancies") sys.exit(1) if __name__ == "__main__": main()

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Expected First Division table — Saturday 28 September 1974

Reference table used to verify `data/input/week-10-results.csv` (the frozen
"week 10" cutoff: all First Division matches played on or before Sat 28 Sep
1974, per `docs/requirements.md`).

## Source

- **Statto** (statto.com, site defunct since 2017), page titled
  *"English Division One (old) Table on Saturday 28th September 1974"*,
  via the Wayback Machine:
  `http://web.archive.org/web/20160401123205/http://www.statto.com/football/stats/england/division-one-old/1974-1975/table/full/1974-09-28`
- Accessed: 2026-07-11.

The originally planned source, `https://www.11v11.com/league-tables/league-division-one/28-September-1974/`,
was unreachable at access time: the live site returns HTTP 403 to
non-browser clients, that URL has no Wayback snapshot, and a save-page
request to the Wayback Machine failed (HTTP 520). See "Independent
cross-check" below for how 11v11 was still used.

## Table as shown by the source

| Pos | Team | P | W | D | L | GF | GA | GAvg | Pts |
|----:|------|--:|--:|--:|--:|---:|---:|-----:|----:|
| 1 | Ipswich Town | 10 | 8 | 0 | 2 | 18 | 6 | 3.00 | 16 |
| 2 | Manchester City | 10 | 6 | 2 | 2 | 14 | 11 | 1.27 | 14 |
| 3 | Liverpool | 10 | 6 | 1 | 3 | 17 | 8 | 2.12 | 13 |
| 4 | Everton | 10 | 4 | 5 | 1 | 14 | 11 | 1.27 | 13 |
| 5 | Sheffield United | 10 | 5 | 3 | 2 | 14 | 14 | 1.00 | 13 |
| 6 | Newcastle United | 9 | 5 | 2 | 2 | 16 | 13 | 1.23 | 12 |
| 7 | Middlesbrough | 9 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 12 | 7 | 1.71 | 11 |
| 8 | Derby County | 10 | 3 | 5 | 2 | 16 | 13 | 1.23 | 11 |
| 9 | Stoke City | 10 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 13 | 11 | 1.18 | 11 |
| 10 | Wolverhampton Wndrs | 10 | 3 | 5 | 2 | 12 | 11 | 1.09 | 11 |
| 11 | Carlisle United | 10 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 8 | 8 | 1.00 | 10 |
| 12 | West Ham United | 10 | 4 | 1 | 5 | 20 | 18 | 1.11 | 9 |
| 13 | Burnley | 10 | 4 | 1 | 5 | 17 | 18 | 0.94 | 9 |
| 14 | Birmingham City | 10 | 3 | 2 | 5 | 12 | 17 | 0.71 | 8 |
| 15 | Coventry City | 10 | 2 | 4 | 4 | 11 | 17 | 0.65 | 8 |
| 16 | Leicester City | 9 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 13 | 17 | 0.77 | 7 |
| 17 | Luton Town | 10 | 1 | 5 | 4 | 11 | 16 | 0.69 | 7 |
| 18 | Chelsea | 10 | 2 | 3 | 5 | 10 | 18 | 0.56 | 7 |
| 19 | Leeds United | 9 | 2 | 2 | 5 | 12 | 14 | 0.86 | 6 |
| 20 | Arsenal | 9 | 2 | 2 | 5 | 9 | 12 | 0.75 | 6 |
| 21 | Tottenham Hotspur | 9 | 3 | 0 | 6 | 11 | 15 | 0.73 | 6 |
| 22 | Queens Park Rangers | 10 | 1 | 4 | 5 | 8 | 13 | 0.61 | 6 |

Unequal Played counts (9 vs 10) are correct: six clubs had a postponed or
not-yet-played fixture as of the cutoff.

## Verification performed (2026-07-11, `scripts/audit_results.py`)

1. **Aggregates vs this table**: P, W, D, L, GF, GA, Pts computed from
   `data/input/week-10-results.csv` match this table exactly — all 22 teams,
   all 7 columns, zero discrepancies.
2. **Independent cross-check (different site)**: because results and this
   table share a source (Statto), the dataset's prefix up to 7 Sep 1974
   (66 matches) was additionally compared against 11v11's archived table
   dated 7 September 1974
   (`http://web.archive.org/web/20201213215254/https://www.11v11.com/league-tables/league-division-one/07-september-1974/`,
   accessed 2026-07-11): exact match, all 22 teams, all 7 columns.
3. **Sanity anchors from prior research** (docs/assignment research):
   Ipswich Town top with 8 wins from 10, two points clear of Manchester
   City; QPR, Tottenham, Arsenal and Leeds level on 6 points at the
   bottom — all confirmed by this table.

## Naming note

Statto spells one club "Wolverhampton Wndrs" (11v11: "Wolverhampton
Wanderers"). The input CSV currently uses the Statto spelling; the
canonical display name is an open decision for the CSV contract.

content.text:
Cycle 1 complete — stopped before cycle 2, as instructed. `./mvnw verify` is fully green (2 tests, 0 Spotless/Checkstyle violations).

**RED** — `StandingsCalculatorTest.singleMatchYieldsWinnerAndLoserRecords` asserting "Liverpool 3, Arsenal 1" produces exactly your two specified records. Ran it alone: failed with `cannot find symbol` on `MatchResult`, `TeamRecord`, `StandingsCalculator` — the failure proves none of the behaviour existed.

**GREEN** — minimum implementation, 3 new files in `com.spandigital.standings.domain` (no CSV, no CLI, no comparator):
- `MatchResult` — immutable record: `homeTeam, homeScore, awayTeam, awayScore` (1 line of substance).
- `TeamRecord` — immutable record: `team, played, won, drawn, lost, goalsFor, goalsAgainst`, with `points()` **derived** as `won * 2 + drawn` rather than stored — one less invariant to violate.
- `StandingsCalculator.calculate(List<MatchResult>) → List<TeamRecord>` — emits one record per team per match via a single `teamRecord(team, scored, conceded)` helper used symmetrically for home and away. Deliberately does *not* accumulate multiple matches per team yet — that's cycle 2's failing test, not free functionality.

**REFACTOR** — reviewed and made no changes: records already give immutability and value equality (which the test exploits via `containsExactlyInAnyOrder`), the calculator is stateless/`final`, names are clean. The Spotless hook auto-formatted each file on write, so formatting never entered the picture.

Diff summary: 4 new files, 67 lines total —

```
src/main/java/…/domain/MatchResult.java          3 lines
src/main/java/…/domain/TeamRecord.java           8 lines
src/main/java/…/domain/StandingsCalculator.java 27 lines
src/test/java/…/domain/StandingsCalculatorTest.java 29 lines
```

One observation for a later cycle: `SmokeTest` is now redundant scaffolding; I've left it alone (removing tests needs your call). Ready for cycle 2 — accumulation across multiple matches — on your go.

message.content:
Good cycle. Commit now: "test: define single-match scoring behaviour" then "feat: calculate team records from a match result" if you kept red and green as separable changes — otherwise one commit "feat: calculate team records from match results via TDD". Push after.

On SmokeTest: remove it in the next commit that touches tests — its job (proving the pipeline runs tests) is done and it's now noise. Note the removal rationale in the commit message.

Then proceed through cycles 2, 3 and 4 with the same red-green-refactor discipline, committing at each green milestone:

Cycle 2 — a draw: both teams played+1, drawn+1, 1 point each, mirrored goals.
Cycle 3 — away win and accumulation: away winner gets the 2 points; a team appearing in multiple matches accumulates (played, W/D/L, goals) rather than duplicating records; input order does not affect totals.
Cycle 4 — invariants as property-style tests over a small fixture set: played = won+drawn+lost for every team; total GF equals total GA across the table; each processed match raises total played by exactly 2.

Stop after cycle 4 is green and committed. Do NOT start the ranking comparator — cycle 5 gets its own briefing.

content.text:
Cycles 2–4 complete, all committed and pushed; stopped before the ranking comparator as instructed. `./mvnw verify` green: **8 tests, 0 violations**.

**Cycle 1 commits** — kept red/green separable as you suggested: `a1aa16f` (test) then `0035ccb` (feat), pushed. One honest caveat: the standalone test commit doesn't compile by itself, so `git bisect` would treat it as a broken commit — if that bothers you we can squash next time, but history now documents the discipline.

**Cycle 2 (draw)** — `3ecdc30`. The test was green on first run: cycle 1's minimal `scored == conceded` ternary already generalised to draws, so no red state was possible without sabotaging working code. The test pins the behaviour; the commit message says so. SmokeTest removed in this commit with the rationale you specified (its pipeline-proving job is done).

**Cycle 3 (away win + accumulation)** — `8dbcdaa`. Proper red: `Expected size: 3 but was: 4` — Liverpool appeared twice instead of accumulating. Green via `LinkedHashMap.merge` with a `TeamRecord add(total, single)` combiner; the away-win case was already covered (test pins it). Order-independence asserted by comparing normal vs. reversed input as sets. Also caught a real workflow quirk: the Spotless hook stripped my imports between two edits (they were momentarily unused), causing a compile failure I had to re-fix — worth remembering as AI_REFLECTION material.

**Cycle 4 (invariants)** — `677d903`. New `StandingsInvariantsTest` over an 8-match fixture: `played = W+D+L` for every team; total GF = total GA; and total played rises by exactly 2 per match — checked across *all prefixes* of the fixture, not just the final state. Green on first run, as expected for pinning tests.

Current domain state: `MatchResult` and `TeamRecord` records (points derived, never stored), `StandingsCalculator` accumulating per team keyed by first appearance. Still deliberately absent: comparator/ranking, CSV, CLI. Ready for the cycle 5 briefing.

message.content:
Cycle 5: the ranking comparator — the highest-risk logic in the project. Authoritative spec: docs/rules-research.md §3-5 and §8. Strict TDD, and I want the RED set designed before any implementation.

RED — write ALL of these as failing tests first (parameterized where it aids clarity), show me the suite failing:
1. Higher points ranks first, regardless of goal record.
2. Equal points: higher goal average ranks first — MUST include the counter-example where goal difference disagrees: A with GF20/GA10 (GD+10, GAve 2.000) ranks ABOVE B with GF25/GA14 (GD+11, GAve ~1.786). This test failing under a goal-difference implementation is the point of the test.
3. Equal points AND equal goal average: higher goals-for ranks first (e.g. 20/10 vs 10/5 — identical average, 20 GF wins).
4. Equal on all three: alphabetical by team name ascending.
5. GA=0 edge: GF>0, GA=0 ranks above any finite goal average; between two GA=0 teams, higher GF first.
6. 0/0 record (played but no goals either way, or unplayed team) — explicit, documented placement; must not throw.
7. Comparator contract: transitivity and consistency spot-checks — including that cross-multiplication uses long arithmetic (construct values where int multiplication would overflow, e.g. GF/GA in the tens of thousands — unrealistic for football, correct for the contract).

GREEN — implement as a dedicated StandingsRanking comparator (domain package): integer cross-multiplication per rules-research §4, longs only, no floating point anywhere in comparison logic. Handle the zero cases per §5 explicitly, not by division.

REFACTOR — then run /review on the diff before committing.

Commit separately: "test: define 1974-75 ranking behaviour" / "feat: rank standings by points, goal average, goals for" (or combined if you judge the non-compiling-test-commit caveat from cycle 1 applies). Stop after commit — position assignment (1,2,3... and the tied-positions question) is cycle 6, not this one.

content.text: Cycle 5, RED first — designing the full failing suite before any implementation. The comparator will be `StandingsRanking implements Comparator<TeamRecord>` where `compare(a,b) < 0` means *a ranks above b* (natural sort = table order). A `assertRanksAbove` helper asserts both directions, so every pairwise test also checks sign-antisymmetry:
